coredata.dc:description: To enable thermal behavior prediction and design optimization of molten salt reactors, thermal conductivity of molten salts must be characterized in terms of salt composition and temperature. Current theoretical models fail to provide consistent approximations for all halide mixtures, particularly actinide-bearing melts. This study aims to link the short-range order structure of molten salts to the mean free path of energy carriers through a simple structural coherence model informed by the partial pair distribution function. The proposed method is used to predict the thermal conductivity of 33 alkali and alkaline earth halide salts. Predictions approximate experimental measurements with a mean absolute error of 15.7% for dissociating, complexing, and actinide salts, including unary LiCl, NaCl, and MgCl2 as well as mixtures LiF–NaF–KF (FLiNaK), LiF–BeF2 (FLiBe), and NaCl–UCl3. The work provides evidence for the validity of energy carrier descriptions of molecular-level heat transfer in molten salts, with implications for improved theories of liquid energy transport in general.

Meaningful discussions with Benjamin Frandsen and Matthew Asplund also provided valuable direction for the project. The authors are also grateful for the assistance of Noah Giauque, Britton Baltich, Isaac Cutler, and Lizzy Robinson in compiling and interpreting data for this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To enable thermal behavior prediction and design optimization of molten salt reactors, thermal conductivity of molten salts must be characterized in terms of salt composition and temperature. Current theoretical models fail to provide consistent approximations for all halide mixtures, particularly actinide-bearing melts. This study aims to link the short-range order structure of molten salts to the mean free path of energy carriers through a simple structural coherence model informed by the partial pair distribution function. The proposed method is used to predict the thermal conductivity of 33 alkali and alkaline earth halide salts. Predictions approximate experimental measurements with a mean absolute error of 15.7% for dissociating, complexing, and actinide salts, including unary LiCl, NaCl, and MgCl2 as well as mixtures LiF–NaF–KF (FLiNaK), LiF–BeF2 (FLiBe), and NaCl–UCl3. The work provides evidence for the validity of energy carrier descriptions of molecular-level heat transfer in molten salts, with implications for improved theories of liquid energy transport in general.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The PDF analysis and structural coherence calculation codes are openly available in 2026-Structural-Coherence- Model at https://github.com/BYU-TEMP-Lab/2026-Structural-Coherence-Model, Ref. [163].

ce:section.ce:para: To enable the use of molten salts as a coolant and nuclear fuel carrier in Gen IV molten salt reactors (MSRs) [1], reactor parameterization requires improved models of microscale thermal transport in the liquid salt. Salts of interest vary widely, including Terrapower’s homogeneous chloride actinide-bearing mixtures [2], fluoride coolant salts in Kairos Power’s pebble bed reactor [3], and thorium and uranium-bearing fluoride salts used by Copenhagen Atomics [4]. Predicting the transient response of the MSR to dynamic variations in temperature and the evolution of fission and corrosion product concentrations relies on a thorough characterization of temperature- and composition-dependent thermophysical properties. Combined with the range of salt compositions considered, the inherent difficulties of measuring molten salt properties make their direct tabulation prohibitive—particularly true for thermal conductivity. This work addresses the lack of thermal conductivity characterization by developing a structurally-based model of molecular energy transport.

Thermal conductivity, denoted by k , is a vital parameter for establishing safe operational windows and designing efficient heat exchange systems in MSRs [5–7]. Measurements of k are challenged by electrical interference, instrument corrosion, and unaccounted convective and radiative heat transfer contributions. Still absent is a standardized measurement method that has been shown to universally address these confounding effects [8]. Reported measurement uncertainties range from 1–20 % for a variety of methods [9], though critical evaluation of the literature values included in the thermophysical arm of the Molten Salt Thermal Properties Database (MSTDB-TP, version 3.1) applies conservative assignments of uncertainty up to 25 %
[10]. Only 26 entries for thermal conductivity out of 820 salt compositions are listed in the MSTDB-TP, of which just five are actinide-bearing mixtures. The lack of data for actinide-bearing mixtures forms a significant barrier to the predictability required for broad characterization and optimization of fueled salt mixtures for various MSR designs [11].

Parallel efforts to inform the thermophysical characterization of molten salts, alongside experimental measurements, are supported by a growing theoretical understanding of liquid thermal conductivity. Ideally, a comprehensive liquid thermal theory can serve to cross-validate the limited and uncertain measurements. In contrast to well-developed theories for solids and gases, however, the fundamental mechanisms of liquid energy transport are obscured by complex interactions that obfuscate conventional approaches from the solid and gaseous phases. Significant questions still under debate involve the dependency of molten salt thermal conductivity on temperature, ion mass, and complexation. Nearly all theories predict a negative temperature dependence related to the thermal expansion of the liquid pseudo-lattice [12], though conflicting experimental results are somewhat inconclusive in confirming this hypothesis [13]. Composition-dependent models are typically based on thermodynamic properties of salt compounds, often with loose reference to the molten salt structure. To expand the modeling capacity of thermal transport theory in liquids, a more direct connection must be established between the molecular structure of liquids and their related modes of energy transfer.

To support MSR deployment through more informed energy transport models, a model of thermal conductivity linked to the liquid structure is developed. Prior theories of k are reviewed to identify shortcomings of current mechanistic descriptions. A phenomenological description of thermal conductivity is introduced based on an approximation of the level of structural coherence for energy carriers, with parameters informed by the pair distribution function (PDF) of molten salts. Comparisons of the presented model to experimental data and established theories demonstrate improved accuracy and applicability to both pseudo-unary and multi-component molten salts, including actinide-bearing mixtures. The results contribute to the growing predictive capability of molten salt technologies and provide broader insight into the mechanisms of liquid energy transport.

This section presents the state of the art of existing theories on the thermal conductivity of molten salt. It includes a discussion on energy carriers in the liquid followed by a comparison of several classes of models built on dual-phase, kinetic, phononic, or structurally-based models.

Establishing a tractable theory of liquid thermal conductivity ultimately leads to the problem of characterizing the excitations—the energy carriers—that mediate thermal transport. The nature of the energy carriers of liquids is still under debate. Despite the complex interactions involved in the liquid phase, notable observations from experimental and computational approaches have contributed to an improved qualitative understanding of the dominant participants in liquid molecular energy transfer.

Significant research in recent decades sheds light on the role of vibrational mechanisms in liquid thermal transport as opposed to ion diffusion [14]. Definitions invoked for these processes range from harmonic coupling of non-propagating atomic vibrations limited to the Ioffe-Regel range of interatomic distances (diffusons) [15,16] up to long-range collective vibrational modes (phonons) [17,18]. The large Lorentz number of molten salts suggests that thermal conduction by vibrational processes dominates over diffusion of charged ions [19]. Turnbull reported for molten salts NaNO3 and NaCl that diffusion contributes less than 5 % to the total conduction [20]. Accordingly, liquids are more similar to solids because they maintain a shear-sustaining structure at small time and length scales, although they do differ in the variable phase space available to phonons [21]. What remains unresolved is the relative contributions of nearest neighbor transfer lengths versus longer-range paths of phonon-like modes, if such are prevalent in liquids.

Computational approaches enable unique resolution of the liquid picture. Since the earliest applications of computer modeling, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations have been used to calculate the complex interatomic interactions and provide meaningful predictions of molten salt structure, vibrational modes, and transport properties [22–24], as extensively reviewed by Porter et al. [25]. The application of MD simulations using ab-initio methods or density functional theory (DFT) requires no empirical fitting per se but necessitates approximations of interatomic potentials that are often informed by measurement results.

Establishing liquid thermal theory requires more than simulations alone. Direct MD calculations of thermal conductivity using Green-Kubo analysis involve significant uncertainties in Onsager proportionality constants and sensitivity to simulation size and time [26,27]. Obtaining thermal conductivity from MD requires 1–2 orders of magnitude more time than extracting simple atomic arrangements and density, especially in ab-initio models where the number of basis functions and computational costs increase with more fundamental first-principles calculations of varying salt mixtures at different temperatures. Machine learned potentials have increased the computational capacity of MD simulations for actinide-bearing mixtures using reasonable data [28,29], though employing deep machine learning has a limited transferability between differing salt mixtures [25]. Informing machine learning models with relevant physics has shown improved transport property predictions of, for example, KCl [30]. This emphasizes the importance of clarifying liquid energy transport to accelerate modeling of salt mixtures in general and more rapidly address data scarcity.

Predictive models of molten salt thermal conductivity vary in their dependence on empirical parameterization. Fitting methods such as the corresponding states method [31,32], semi-empirical models, and application of standard reference correlations [9] are limited by high uncertainty and availability of measurement data. Approaches using the Redlich-Kister formalism to account for non-ideal mixing behavior have shown notable success for both density and viscosity predictions [33,34]. However, application of the formalism to thermal conductivity is impaired due to a less direct connection to excess functions as well as the sparse pseudobinary and pseudoternary mixture data. Given the uncertainty in thermal conductivity measurements, extrapolated fitting models based on partial datasets should be supported by a theoretical framework for validation.

Theoretical approaches originate from a spectrum of solid to gas-like frameworks, and the earliest of these explore liquid transport from one of these two phases. Chapman and Enskog’s models adapt a gas description toward liquid densities with a collisional cross-section fitted to experimental viscosity, but fail for multicomponent mixtures without a connection to intermolecular forces and structural arrangements [35–38]. Eyring’s significant structure theory separated liquid transport into a dual gas-solid model by differentiating the contributions of solid lattice vibrations from traveling quasiparticles in the form of gas-like holes of free energy [39]. The more recent work of Trachenko and Brazhkin connected the evidence of phonon modes in liquids [40,41] to heat capacity and other transport properties [17,42–44]. Their work theorizes solid-like vibrational modes among liquid molecules vibrating around semi-equilibrium positions when above the high-frequency Frenkel limit [45], providing a path for predictive models informed by aspects of phonon energy transfer. The contributions of these models show that some solid-like descriptions work in limited liquid scenarios, but the use of the terms for diffusons and, especially, phonons in the liquid context remains nuanced and largely conceptual for engineering approximations.

While a direct translation of the crystalline solid phonon formalism to the amorphous and then liquid phase is barred by the lack of long-range order [46], the evident short-range order of salts [47,48] provides a basis for the possible contributions of correlated energy transfer at short-time scales, dependent on the probability of structural order. Conceptions of a quasi-lattice lack evidence in liquids and even glasses [49]; however, even a significantly limited ordered range beyond a single interatomic distance can enable doubling of the mean free path if such transient structures are probable in the liquid salt. The degree of this effect has yet to be distinguished in molten salt models. Sections 2.2.2 and 2.2.3 discuss the limitations imposed by this lack of resolution in current molten salt thermal conductivity models, with Section 2.2.5 reviewing the evidential support for a refined structural interpretation of liquid energy transfer.

First derived for monatomic gases, the original kinetic theory expresses macroscopic thermal conductivity in terms of volumetric specific heat ( c ), average velocity ( v ), and mean free path ( ℓ ):

Eq. (1) fundamentally describes energy carrier-mediated thermal conductivity. This is why the expression is used analogously for different types of energy carriers. Gheribi et al. proposed a kinetic theory model (abbreviated KTM) [12,27], where diffusons are named as the dominant energy carriers given their prevalence in amorphous solids [50]. Outside of actinide mixtures, the KTM predicts within experimental accuracy for a wide array of unary and higher-order salts (binary, ternary, etc.) [51].

Some of the KTM’s modifying functions present inconsistencies in modeled effects. The complexation rate ζ ( x , T ) is a ratio of the mass of free ions and complexes to the average molecular weight as obtained from MD simulations, equal to 1 for completely dissociating mixtures and increasing with the degree of complex formation [27]. Zhao’s phonon gas model [37] (PGM) uses a basis similar to the KTM by applying the kinetic theory to a “phonon gas”, but attempts to characterize effective phonon properties using macroscopic properties and directly relate macroscopic properties to phonon characteristics. Building on Trachenko’s phononic descriptions of liquid heat capacity, the PGM starts by lumping the frequency-dependent expression of phonon thermal conductivity in crystals into an effective gray description:

The liquid sound velocity is inferred by experimental evidence to closely approximate the group velocity given the similarity of the dispersion of liquid argon acoustic modes to its sound velocity [52–54]. The PGM assumes the mean free path to be the minimum transfer distance, captured by the average sum of ionic radii (
d
avg
) for the case of molten salts composed of non-uniformly sized ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of kinetic and phonon gas models
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 provides a comparison of the relative KTM and PGM prediction accuracy, showing the mean absolute error (MAE) and R2 of the linear fit for each model as applied to a range of actinide and non-actinide-bearing salts. Due to the KTM’s requirement of MD-informed complexation structures to construct ζ , all predictions were made assuming ζ = 1 . Even without applying ζ , which could only further dampen the local kinetic energy available for transfer, most KTM predictions underpredict the experimental results. This is consistently the case for actinide-bearing mixtures, which are underestimated with a 57.9 % MAE. In contrast, the KTM predicts non-actinide salts with 17.1 % MAE. The discrepancy suggests that the mass fluctuation factor should play a more limited role in reducing the thermal conductivity than what is currently incorporated in Eq. (4).

By comparison, the PGM maintains similar results for both actinide and non-actinide-bearing mixtures, with an MAE roughly half that of the KTM predictions for actinides (24.1%). The similarity of the linear fits for both salt groups using the PGM shown in Fig. 1 suggests that for the parameters invoked by Eq. (5), the same mechanisms accounting for inaccuracies in non-actinide salts persist in actinide-bearing mixtures.

The KTM assumes the ideal thermal conductivity as described by ion complexity, density, and sound velocity must be dampened by complexation and thermodynamic factors. The KTM begins with a minimum thermal conductivity expression, though, at the melting temperature, this actually represents the maximum thermal conductivity the prediction can attain because all modifiers decrease this value. When T =
T 0
, Eq. (2) resolves to the prefactor that contains the kinetic theory expression (the kinetic theory part in Eq. (2)), where ψ
k B
n represents the volumetric specific heat involved in heat transfer,
v s
is the carrier velocity, and
n
− 1
/
3
is the mean free path. This expression is then dampened by the temperature-dependent part of Eq. (2) and the compositionally-dependent complexation of (3). These inhibiting functions do not explicitly modify any one particular parameter in the prefactor, making it difficult to track which are effectively being amplified or reduced.

Zhao’s PGM confines its predictability solely to variations in the three parameters assumed to be captured by
c p
,
v s
, and
d
avg
in Eq. (5). The simple expression provides a valuable starting point to evaluate the accuracy of these parameter assignments and the overall validity of kinetic theory, which describes thermal conductivity by characterizing the participating energy carriers.

Because both models originate from the fundamental kinetic theory of energy carriers, distinctions between the results of the KTM and PGM are instructive. Fig. 2 presents a comparison of the correlations of the mean free path, specific heat, and velocity terms as calculated by both models. While an ideal model is not expected to have a linear fit for individual parameters when some degree of coupling may exist between them, the figure shows the relative deviation between methods of calculation and the expected variability of k attributable to each. As a general trend, the specific heat and velocity terms show a positive correlation with experimental thermal conductivity, which makes physical sense according to Eq. (1). Although an increased mean free path contributes to increased thermal conductivity, the opposite is observed for both models in Fig. 2(a) and (b) due to the dominant specific heat and velocity factors. Because the mean free path defined by the PGM is generally lower than that of the KTM, this may explain why the linear fits for PGM in Fig. 1 increasingly deviate as the experimental thermal conductivity increases. The KTM correlation for mean free path displays a similar slope but shifted up, providing the kinetic thermal conductivity of Eq. (2) with a higher starting point for subsequent dampening functions.

This rough approximation excludes covariance components by assuming term independence, which ignores the influence of density on each term, for example. However, this is sufficient for the purposes of comparing the two models’ relative interpretation of each term’s contribution. A summary of the percentage contributions for the PGM and KTM is given in Table 1 (applied to the dataset plotted in Fig. 1).

Table 1 shows the mean free path to be the least participating term for both models, especially so for the KTM’s

= 14.8 % . Both models conceptually establish the mean free path to be the minimum interaction distance, similar to all previous kinetic theory-based models of liquid thermal conductivity [14]. The PGM’s
d

KTM’s low sensitivity and positive bias for the mean free path may partly account for its relative success even when excluding the average sum of radii, allowing the other terms and thermodynamic modifiers to dominate variability. The KTM’s current usage of a mean free path obtained from the number density is proposed to represent the minimum distance of half the wavelength of a phonon as constrained by the lattice size [27,76]. The interatomic distance defined by the PGM’s
d
avg
restricts a phonon gas to the minimum wavelengths that can sustain a phonon definition.

Ambiguity in the mean free path term stems from unknown levels of structural compatibility with phonon modes. In a lattice of particles in semi-equilibrium positions, the Brillouin zone boundary constraining the minimum space for a wave has a minimum wave vector magnitude of

Despite the conceptual use of this threshold for the KTM and PGM, the values used to represent it vary significantly between models. Diffusons are meant to describe the mean free path term of the KTM with

by anywhere between 7–57% for the dataset of 23 unary halide salts (including actinide and non-actinides). This is due to the fact that salt ions are not uniformly sized or distributed. The range of variation leads the mean free path to stand out as a particular point of uncertainty, particularly when the percent contribution of the mean free path is less than 15% for both models. Neither model proposes a mean free path greater than the anticipated minimum interaction distance, despite the fact that an idealized phonon path could extend beyond the lattice constant depending on the finite (even if low) probability of a coherent phonon path.

Table 1 indicates the large contribution of the specific heat term in the KTM with
P
sp
= 76.7 % , which establishes itself as the dominant factor that is then subject to the modifying functions. The PGM’s use of the measured volumetric specific heat of the salt

/

differs from the KTM’s quantity ψ
k B
n by anywhere between −57% and 67% (for the same group of actinide and non-actinide halides). Interestingly, for unary salts, the PGM is lower than the KTM by an average of −43%, while for mixtures the KTM exceeds the PGM by 45%. This points to a potential misrepresentation of the material constant ψ , which decreases with the complexity of the ion or interionic complexes. This reduces the calculated heat capacity term despite the increased degrees of freedom generally available with complexation. This runs counter to the positive correlation of specific heat with complexation observed in the larger-scale case of enhanced specific heat in molten salts with dendrite-forming nanoparticles [77], though it may be that some energy storage modes within ions or extended complexes transfer at much lower velocities.

The KTM utilizes the Grüneisen parameter, calculated by γ = ρ α
v s 2
/
c p
(where ρ is mass density in [ kg
/
m 3
] ) to dampen the kinetic contributions to thermal conductivity via increased phonon scattering [27]. If the mean free path is already fixed at the minimum half-wavelength distance per the formalism of the KTM, this term loses its relevance. The intended effect of γ on the parameters in Eq. (1) is obscured by the model’s general underestimation of the involved volumetric specific heat compared to the PGM’s
1 3
c p
, suggesting sound velocity to be the principal parameter targeted according to the formalism.

For both models, the energy carrier velocity is given by the measured adiabatic sound velocity of the molten salt. Zhao noted the absence of frequency-dependent group velocity data, which may portend transfer velocities of short-wavelength phonons differing from the sound velocity formed by long-wavelength density fluctuations.

The thermodynamic relationships in the KTM model introduced a valuable thermodynamically-based temperature dependency, even if the specific parameters affected stand to be further clarified. Temperature variation in the PGM is given by the individual temperature-dependencies of each parameter in Eq. (5). Similarly, compositional dependence of the PGM can be described by the mixture’s bulk specific heat, sound velocity, and average sum of atomic radii. The KTM’s compositional variation functions may be overcompensating for complexation effects, as appears to be the case for actinide mixtures. Further understanding of the liquid structure’s effect on kinetic theory parameters is needed to verify the compositional dependency.

The PGM is valuable for its explicit evaluation of individual parameters in the kinetic theory’s thermal conductivity expression. Accurate characterization of energy carriers’ attributes in terms of heat capacity, transfer velocity, and mean free path is necessary before modifiers are applied to the overall conductivity. Such resolution can clarify potential mechanistic contributions, permitting evaluation of individual parameter contributions to the variability of liquid thermal conductivity.

The performance of the KTM and PGM highlights the particular difficulty of defining the mean free path of the dominant energy carrier in molten salts. In his review of molecular-level energy transport models in liquids, Chen noted that the thermal conductivities of solids can vary by 4 orders of magnitude, while those of non-metallic liquids fall within a narrow range [14]. Phonon descriptions in liquids imply a significant reduction in the mean free path due to total loss of long-range order and dramatic drops in relaxation times of the liquid lattice, drastically reducing the thermal conductivity of liquids compared to solids. Mean free path variation in the liquid regime, however, may extend beyond the minimum interaction distance, as suggested by the general underestimation of the PGM when using the lower limit
d
avg
. If collective vibrational modes form a non-negligible contribution, more structural information is required to estimate the degree to which these may contribute to the mean free path in the molten salts.

The issues with liquid structure allowing coherent propagation of energy carriers are two-fold: liquids lack long-range order, and the structure that exists is sustained only over a small relaxation time. X-ray and neutron scattering experiments have been used to observe the microscopic structure of materials for nearly a century [78], including for liquids such as molten salts [79]. Applying a Fourier transform to the scattering pattern can reveal short-range structural correlations, allowing calculation of the pair distribution function (PDF). Taking a step back from previous models, phonon definitions can be neglected by focusing on quantifying the degree of structural coherence that determines the participation of collective modes.

The PDF, defined as g ( r ) , is a probability density function giving the likelihood of finding pairs of atoms separated by a distance r. For multi-component liquids such as molten salt mixtures, the partial PDF
g
A B
( r ) is most useful, which gives the probability distributions of atom B around atom A. The partial PDF is most commonly extracted from MD simulations whose back-calculated total PDF is then validated by the experimental results. In principle,
g
A B
( r ) can also be obtained experimentally through isotopic substitution of the mixture components [80], though the process is especially time-consuming and costly.

While the PDF provides a time-averaged snapshot of liquid structure, the real-space and time Van Hove correlation function (VHF) provides a time-dependent description of the short-time frame local dynamics of liquid atoms [81]. The VHF can therefore capture the relaxation behaviors in molten salts and metallic liquids [82,83]. Whether the mean free path is predominantly limited by the structural or temporal behavior of the liquid has been unclear. Due to the lack of VHF data, the current work principally analyzes the PDF to clarify the structural effect on the mean free path.

The probability density given by the PDF also corresponds to the strength of interaction potentials between ions, which can be used to quantify relative interaction factors. The potential of mean force, as calculated by W ( r ) = −
k B
T ln ⁡
g ( r )
, can be superimposed over the PDF to describe the average radial-dependence of intermolecular forces, informing positions of energy minima and relative magnitudes [84]. The features of the first peak distance for ion pairs, in particular, encapsulate the effect of the energy minima on the number density of neighboring ions, as depicted for NaCl in Fig. 3 where the minimum occurs at the first Na–Cl peak at r = 2.67 Å. These methods resemble approaches used to estimate self-diffusion coefficients of liquids using the PDF [85]. Contributing factors to structural coherence and energy transfer can be scaled by the relative position and magnitude of this first peak and subsequent minima, as will be discussed in Section 3. By defining these interaction factors as relative ratios rather than absolute quantities, the PDF can enhance prediction accuracy using the formalism described below.

Using the PDF to inform structural effects on energy propagation assumes that the limited-range PDF contains sufficient information to quantify the mean free path. By fitting a power law to the first 4 peak positions of the cation anion pairs of 13 alkali molten salt PDFs [86–89], all achieve R2
> 99 % with an average mean relative error of 0.72 % , which indicates that the factors contributing to structural disorder progress at a predictable rate as a function of radius. This observation suggests that quantifying the degradation of structural coherence within 2–4 coordination spheres given by PDF data provides a reasonable understanding of radially-dependent decoherence factors.

While the time-averaged snapshot of the PDF does not show how the correlations change with time (as is given in the VHF), an additional time dependent decoherence factor would act as a modification to the structural decoherence, depending on which dominates. If the structural decoherence primarily suppresses energy carrier propagation before relaxation of the structure occurs, the time dependence may be negligible. In any case, calculating the structure should provide an overestimate of the average distance traveled by energy carriers. As will be discussed, time dependence is shown to have a minimal effect compared to the structural disorder in molten salts and is left as a further element to be integrated into more rigorous calculations of the structural coherence length.

The purpose of this paper is to demonstrate a direct connection between the liquid structure and mean free path for use in a phenomenological model of energy carriers analogous to kinetic theory. This work builds on the development of Gheribi [27] and Zhao [37] by assuming the measured specific heat and sound velocity accurately represent energy carrier heat capacity and group velocity. An engineering approximation is developed for the mean free path in terms of structural coherence, informed by the pair distribution function. This approach implicitly accounts for the range of interparticle interactions and collective modes that may contribute to the effective mean free path, hence the reference to general energy carriers. To the authors’ best knowledge, this is the first thermal conductivity model to explicitly incorporate PDF-informed structural information to predict the mean free path. The presented model includes temperature and compositional dependencies. The resulting predictions of the model are then compared to experimental data. The accuracy of the model in capturing physical mechanisms is weighed by its relative success in approximating a range of salt types, including unary and higher-order mixtures, dissociating and complexing salts, and actinide-bearing mixtures. Outcomes of this study may help to elucidate the factors contributing to the mechanism of thermal conductivity in molten salts as well as liquids in general.

The proposed model builds on the fundamental premise of statistical physics that the properties of any interacting system are governed by its excitations [21,90]. From this, two ensuing principles are observed in the context of thermal transport in an interacting medium: (1) heat is transferred by some form of energy carrier, and (2) the effective properties of these energy carriers remain constant while the propagating medium remains coherent. This formalism then requires characterizing the energy carrier(s) and the degree to which the medium maintains coherence in time and space. In this sense, the mean free path of the energy carriers is more aptly described by the effective structural coherence length. This is the average distance over which the medium’s structure permits energy carriers to propagate with relatively constant energy and velocity. The two terms will be used synonymously in the current work. By linking a system’s macroscopic properties to its participating excitations, the degree to which structural stability permits coherent propagation can be used to calculate the thermal conductivity of the system.

To bridge the gap between the macroscopic transport properties and molecular structure of the liquid, the thermal conductivity is first expressed in terms of energy carrier properties. The general description of carrier-mediated thermal conductivity is given by the energy flux J as calculated by the average transport of energy carriers across a plane at position x , given the average velocity v , average carrier energy
E c
, and mean free path ℓ :
(8)

is the number density of energy carriers. This transfer of energy via carriers can be related to the flow of thermal energy by first assuming the internal energy U is equal to the sum of energies of all carriers involved, or
U V

Noting that (10) is an expression of Fourier’s law, the prefactor defines the thermal conductivity in terms of the energy carrier properties:
(11)

This derivation is equivalent to the kinetic theory of gases applied to generalized energy carriers, relying on three assumptions: the energy carriers possess energy proportional to internal energy, the density of energy carriers is equal to the atom density (
n c
= n ), and the group velocity and heat capacity are equivalent across the thermal gradient. The purpose of deriving it here is to emphasize the relationship between properties of the energy carriers and bulk thermal conductance. If the effective heat capacity and group velocity of the generalized energy carriers and the structural coherence length of the medium are known, then Eq. (11) provides an accurate relationship to k . Stated another way, if
c p
, v , and ℓ are accurately known, no other adjusting terms are needed to calculate k .

The following sections describe the formalism for approximating each of these terms using available macroscopic property measurements and the PDF of the molten salts. The terms for measured specific heat and velocity follow the applications of previous models, particularly the PGM. The key improvement of this work is to propose a more informed estimation of the mean free path term. The resulting model provides a calculation of thermal conductivity as a function of composition and temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of coherent medium and energy carriers
ce:section.ce:para: The current work presents a generalized energy carrier model by assuming an ideal energy carrier with a limiting medium. The properties of the dominant energy carrier in a given medium depend on the nature of the principal interacting forces of the system. Examples of energy carriers in respective media include energy carried by delocalized electrons within metals, vibrational energy via phonons in crystalline lattices, or translational energy via monatomic atoms in a dilute gas. Given the short-range order of ions oscillating about semi-equilibrium positions beyond the Frenkel frequency, the current model assumes collective vibrational modes to form the ideal dominant energy carrier for energy propagation, though such may be limited or restricted to interparticle energy transfer depending on the level of structural disorder. Specific phonon descriptions are withheld in favor of considering the generalized energy carrier characterized by the approximated degree of structural coherence.

Central to the structure of molten salts is the characteristic charge alternation formed by the oppositely charged ions [91], from which a coherent medium must be discerned. Using experimentally validated MD simulations of 20 alkali halide salts with the polarizable Wang-Buckingham model, Walz et al. observed that all cation–anion PDF peaks
r
ca , max
occur 43–56% closer in radial distance than the cation–cation peak
r
cc , max
[86]. PDFs of multi-component mixtures exhibit those same trends [24,92–94]. These patterns highlight the structural role of the cation–anion bond in the transfer of energy carriers. Regarding the strength of these interactions relative to same-sign charged ions, the Debye-Hückel/Yukawa interaction potential can be used to quantify the relative energy stored between pairs of particles in the presence of ionic screening, where the potential energy decreases exponentially with distance. The potential V ( r ) is given by:
(12)
V ( r ) =
q 1
q 2
4 π
ϵ 0
r
exp ⁡ (
−
r
/
λ D
)
where
q 1
and
q 2
are the respective charges of the ions,
ϵ 0
is the dielectric permittivity,
λ D
is the Debye length (characterized by the charge density of the mixture), and r is the separation distance. Using pair distances extracted from the PDFs of 11 molten halide salts [86], the cation–anion potential is 1–2 orders of magnitude greater than either the cation–cation or anion–anion potentials, indicating the dominant role of heteroionic energy transfer.

Although charge alternation is typical, some compositions may enable a potentially supplemental role to be played by cation structures if sufficient correlation exists between cations. This has been observed in networks of Mg–Mg cations in MgCl2, attributed to the polarizability of the chloride ions that form cation chains rather than isolated complexes alone [95]. Additional anomalous effects include increased correlation between Mg
2 +
aggregates with an increase in temperature [48], complicating the straightforward view of density-dependent temperature dependence. Smith et al. noted from MD simulations of NaCl–UCl3 that when a system’s maximum PDF A–A peak occurs at less than twice the distance to the A–B peak, it is likely the result of A–B–A linkage and connected A–A chains. [96]. This motif may be tempered by the presence of “spacer salts”, observed by Emerson et al. in the case of NaCl separating the constituent heavy lanthanide in NaCl–LaCl3 [97]. While homoionic collisions may occur in these structures depending on ion mobility, the still-larger separation distance and repulsive dynamics may entail decreased collisional frequency and energy transfer efficiency. It is yet unclear to what extent cation–cation interactions contribute to the energy carrier propagation medium.

Regarding possible mechanisms for cation–cation participation, it is worth considering whether embedded cation networks can act as an independent pathway or help enhance redistribution of energy transferred between coordination spheres. Such effects may permit more efficient energy storage and recovery of transmitted energy carriers than systems with segregated charge alternation, given the same level of structural disorder. Possible explanations for this effect include tighter phonon density of states due to the regular network of cations, which would decrease energy level spacing and allow vibrational energy to be redistributed more evenly [18]. This is similar to the mechanisms that describe “phonon hopping” between ordered domains separated by uncorrelated barriers, which has been used to predict the thermal conductivity of ionic liquids [98,99]. This points to the possibility of cation chains partially bridging coherent heteroionic chain segments. For the purposes of this model, the cation–anion bond is assumed to form the ideal energy transfer medium and is the only transfer medium considered in the current work. Further development of this model will incorporate the potential assistive or parallel contributions via cation structures, though possible indications of such an effect will be discussed in Section 4.

The current model assumes that the measured volumetric specific heat at constant pressure,
c p
, sufficiently captures the effective transferable energy of energy carriers, following Zhao’s use of the same in the PGM [37]. Utilizing
c p
builds on the successful connection made by Trachenko’s liquid heat capacity model, which linked vibrational states to the transient liquid’s structural properties [42]. A significant nuance inherent in this assumption includes the fact that the experimentally measured specific heat comprises all energy storage modes, including non-propagating and near-zero group velocity modes, and therefore would form an upper limit for the actual heat capacity per energy carrier [100]. This would encompass the contributions from non-propagating optical phonons—out-of-phase oscillations of oppositely charged neighbors—which would contribute to the specific heat but provide little impact on thermal conductivity [101]. The alternating cation–anion structure of molten salts enables optic modes, as observed in alkali halides [102,103]. Depending on the population of near-zero-velocity optic phonons, the measured bulk specific heat may overestimate the effective specific heat representing energy carrier heat storage. Experimentally-derived dispersion relations of NaI revealed weak intensities of optic modes [104], however, suggesting their limited role. Additionally, the modes observed indicate that the optic phonons were propagating in nature. The minimal role of near-zero velocity optic modes supports the use of the measured specific heat to account for the effective energy storage of energy carriers.

Because the volumetric specific heat capacity represents the accessible energy modes in the liquid structure available for transfer, it captures the maximum energy mediated by energy carriers. This relies on the assumption that the density of energy carriers is proportional to the atom density and that energy storage modes predominantly correspond to propagating velocities. The high thermal energy available above the melting temperature of salts fills most vibrational modes evenly, satisfying the equipartition theorem. For this reason, a continuous energy level spacing can be assumed and quantum corrections to vibrational modes are negligible. While the degrees of freedom and the resultant energy storage capacity per molecule remain approximately constant with temperature, the number of molecules and permitted energy carriers per unit volume does change with temperature as density decreases. The well-observed increase in volume with temperature in liquids leads to a decreasing volumetric specific heat with temperature. When measured property data for a mixture is unavailable, the specific heat of the mixture,
c
p
( x ) , can be estimated using the molar fraction of each component and the molar volume (
V m
)
c
p
( x ) =
∑ i
x i
c
p
V m
as used in [55].

The average group velocity of phonon-like vibrational energy excitations has been assumed to be captured by the speed of sound in the medium [37], though the validity of this gray approximation depends on the spectral dependence of group velocity and the distribution of modal energies over phonon frequencies. Measured dispersion relations of LiCl, NaCl, KCl, NaI, and RbBr have revealed group velocities exceeding the hydrodynamic limit of sound velocity at small wavelengths [105–109]. These excess velocities have been described as “fast sound” attributed to partial dynamics of the cation sub-component, though the precise source of the phenomenon is still unclear. Fig. 4 reproduces the dispersion relation for molten NaCl at 1170 K [108,110,111], where the group velocity (
v g
) of the wave is given by the slope of frequency ( ω ) to the magnitude of the wave vector ( Q ), or
v g
=
d ω
d Q
. The adiabatic speed of sound (
v s
) is plotted, along with high-frequency sound speed limit,
v ∞
. As shown in Fig. 4, the group velocity given by the dispersion of NaCl exceeds
v s
for wave vectors up to Q ≈ 12 nm-1. This corresponds to a wavelength limit imposed if the first Na–Cl peak distance of the PDF in Fig. 3 is used as the lattice constant r = 2.67 Å =
a
lattice
, giving 2
a
lattice
=
λ
min
= 0.533 nm, where λ is the phonon wavelength related to the wave vector magnitude by λ =
2 π
/
Q
. The increased phase velocities in the region just below Q =
2
a
lattice
show the elastic “solid-like” behavior of liquids at short time scales [112]. This differs from the dispersion relation of argon, which matches the sound velocity up to the interatomic distance, and upon which Zhao’s postulate is based, namely, that all group velocities match the sound velocity [37]. If high group velocity modes are unique to some molten salts and carry significant energy, this could cause underestimations when using the sound velocity for molten salts in a PGM.

The relationship between faster-than-sound modes and modal energy distribution is still unresolved. As noted by Demmel et al., the dispersion of molten NaCl was found to closely match the corresponding liquid metal, Na [110] (
v
Na
in Fig. 4). The phenomenon was also observed in molten KCl with liquid K [106]. To explain the similarity, Demmel et al. proposed that the polarizability of the Cl
−
ions may permit plasmon-type cation motion against the anion background. This was supposed to give the cation interactions similar dynamics to the liquid metal at short wavelengths [106], a process which may be limited in fields of nonhomogeneous cations or less-polarizable anions like F
−
. Ion polarization was attributed to the presence of optic-like modes [103], though MD simulations of the phenomenon in NaCl noted that these velocities were found to exist only at wavenumbers greater than 1.7
A ˙
− 1
and were dampened too quickly to be considered fast sound [113]. Later experimental data by Demmel et al. also reported significant damping as Q approached the lattice constant [108]. While these studies do indicate the presence of optic phonons, there is limited resolution regarding the distribution of energy mediated by these modes. This makes it difficult to parse how differing phase velocities are related to the dominant energy carrier mediating most of thermal energy, given that the viscoelastic models used to describe the dispersion neglected thermal processes [114].

A rigorous calculation of the frequency-dependent group velocities and component-specific contributions will require a study of the partial dynamic structure factors [108]. Additional resolution could come from dispersion measurements of a multi-component salt to inform whether the same “fast sound” exists in the case of mixtures. If the cation network is responsible for increased propagation frequencies, the cation disparity of mixtures should lead to lower velocities.

The contribution of high phase velocities to thermal transport is likely minimized by the disordered liquid structure. Similar to the limitations on fast sound observed in water [115], the rapid damping of optic and other high-velocity modes can lead to much lower effective velocities due to their short lifetimes [101,112]. If the dispersion relations plotted in Fig. 4 predominantly reflect the wavefront of cation–cation interactions, this may hide slower modes involved in cation–anion interactions, where the spectral weight of energy transferred may be more significant. According to Demmel’s proposed mechanism, including fast sound in the current model would require the assumption that cation–cation interactions contribute significantly to thermal conductivity, which still leaves unresolved the speed of cation–anion interactions. Although resolving this remains beyond the scope of the current work, an estimated deviation is reported in Section 4 in the hypothetical case where fast sound is applied to cation–anion transfer processes for unary substances.

For the purposes of the present study, the proposed model follows Zhao’s assumption that the effective velocity of the dominant energy carriers is given by the sound velocity of the liquid, v =
v s
. Because the model begins with a description of the ideal microscopic energy carrier deduced from macroscopic properties and structural PDF information, the long-range effect of energy carrier propagation in the temporally transient liquid structure is assumed to be due to an effective group velocity that matches the measured speed of sound in the liquid. This assumption is supported by the reduced extent of fast sound dynamics due to damping. This also follows the approach of Larkin and McGaughey in predicting group velocities of disordered solids [116]. The purpose of discussing the potential for higher-velocity modes is to both indicate the need for additional research and provide a possible source of variation that might explain model inaccuracies. These implications will be discussed in Section 4.

The measured sound velocity generally shows a negative trend with increasing temperature [117]. Although greater thermal energy with temperature increases the speeds of individual molecules, the drop in density and bulk modulus tends to dominate in liquids. The temperature-dependent speed of sound in a mixture can be estimated using thermodynamic mixing rules [55] for
v s

and

are the volume and mass fractions of the endmember component, respectively.

Using the framework of an ideal energy carrier with effective heat capacity and propagation velocity, the current work proposes a formalism for quantifying the structural coherence length from the pair distribution function. To lay out this approach, decoherence factors are determined to quantify the probability of an energy carrier remaining coherent as a function of position in the PDF. These factors then feed into a coherence disruption probability function, which is used to describe the spatial decay in a cumulative survival function for the energy carriers as r increases. The area under this curve yields the structural coherence length or mean free path.

Because the current model assumes that the cation–anion bond forms the dominant pathway for energy carriers in the molten salt, the ideal coherent medium is a repeating sequence of oppositely charged ions separated by the ideal separation distance for efficient energy transfer. The ideal transfer distance is approximated by the first cation–anion peak position
r
1
, where the average local energy between the pair is minimized [85], as indicated by the potential of mean force ( W ( r ) ) for Na–Cl in molten NaCl (Fig. 3). Decoherence results in the transfer of energy to new energy carriers with a distinct velocity vector or energy. Interactions that result in decoherence include homoionic interactions in the chain (cation–cation or anion–anion), dissimilar cation or anion species from the alternating sequence, or out-of-phase transfer due to different vibrational frequencies. Using these assumptions, the probability of decoherence can be estimated as a function of radial position by calculating relative factors of decoherence at each transfer distance. Obtaining the effective structural coherence length requires connecting these sources of decoherence to a radially-dependent probability of coherence.

The structural coherence length can be determined if the rate of change in medium coherence with distance is known. If N energy carriers are emitted from an originating source, the number of remaining coherent energy carriers will decrease by dN over a distance dr proportional to the radially-dependent probability of coherence disruption, given by the coherence disruption function β ( r ) :
(13)
dN = − N β ( r ) dr

Solving this first-order ODE for the number of energy carriers Nr remaining at distance r compared to the original initial energy carrier count N0 yields a cumulative survival probability function, denoted by S ( r ) :
(14)

N r

ℓ

For salts composed of multiple cation–anion pairs, each pair contributes to the total structural coherence length proportional to its molar fraction. The pair-specific structural coherence length is denoted as
ℓ
sc ,

, where

and

index the i th cation and j th anion species, respectively. Each

is evaluated according to Eq. (15) for the given pair. The mixture-averaged structural coherence length is given by the weighted sum:

is equal to the endmember molar fraction. The following sections will describe how the coherence disruption function and its composite set of disruption factors are calculated for each cation–anion pair, denoted by the subscript
[ ]

. This subscript is needed to distinguish the cation–anion pair of interest from indexed pairs in the PDF.

From Eq. (15), if β ( r ) is known over the radial distance extending from each source of energy carriers, the average structural coherence length can be accurately calculated. This is the key provided by the pair distribution function, which gives sufficient information to estimate β ( r ) for rapidly decaying short-ordered molten salts. The physical meaning of the coherence disruption function entails that if no disruption factors exist over a unit distance, i.e. if the medium remains perfectly coherent, then no energy carriers are lost. According to Eq. (13), this corresponds to β ( r ) = 0 . Conversely, β ( r ) = ∞ describes an impenetrable barrier that disrupts all energy carriers.

Quantifying disruption factors between 0 and ∞ would require normalizing an absolute calculation of dimensional quantities. This can be simplified by defining disruption factors as ratios between 0 and 1, which induce β ( r ) to approach either 0 or ∞ , respectively. Each disruption factor
b k
is the probability that the factor (indexed by k ) causes medium decoherence.

Because maximum transfer efficiency occurs between ions equally spaced by Δ r , each disruption factor is evaluated only at discrete radii denoted
r m
, where the m th transfer point is located at
r m
= m Δ r (for m = 1 , 2 , 3 , … ). This respects the minimum transfer distance of half the minimum wavelength, making S ( r ) = 1 up until
r 1
as all energy carriers will transfer to the nearest ion. This also causes S ( r ) to remain constant between
r
m − 1
> r >
r m
. Although transfer may occur at nonlinear angles and distances in subsequent ion transitions, the energy lost to vector change or inefficient coupling would be reflected in a reduced S ( r ) between transfer points. Neglecting energy losses between
r m
keeps S ( r ) as an upper bound. A Dirac comb function can be applied to calculate the disruption factors only at integer multiples of Δ r :
(17)
b ~
k
( r ) =
b k

m ∈ Z
δ ( r − m Δ r )
where

( r ) is the combed disruption factor at discrete transfer positions.

To allow multiple independent disruption factors to be equally capable of influencing β ( r ) within the limits of 0 ≤ β ( r ) ≤ ∞ , the overall coherence disruption function should then be the sum of all independent coherence disruption factors. This is similar to the convention used to describe the total capture cross-section as the sum of partial cross-sections in binary nuclear reactions [118]. The coherence disruption function can now be defined where
b k
ranges from 0 (no reduction of coherence) to 1 (complete disruption of the coherent medium), and β ( r ) can be written as:
(18)
β ( r ) =
∑ k

0 .

To quantify how structural features contribute to decoherence, a set of disruption factors
b k
is introduced. Each factor encodes a specific structural mechanism that diminishes coherence at a given transfer distance r . These are calculated together for each path to form a first-order approximation of the radially dependent coherence disruption function β ( r ) .

The generalized energy carrier is modeled as a plane wave propagating along a representative cation–anion chain. Although a real carrier may interact with ions across several lattice spacings, an extended cross-sectional structure is neglected. Also ignored are angular correlations, which are implicitly averaged over in the PDF. These simplifications reduce the problem to estimating coherence along a one-dimensional chain, dependent only on the radial probability distribution of ion species. Higher fidelity considerations of angular dependence and nonlinear transfers are likely to introduce additional mechanisms that reduce coherence or the effective radial transfer distance, leaving the simplified model as an upper-bound estimate.

A collective vibration will be sustained if the ideal structure of alternating cations and anions exists. A non-ideal recipient factor
b
NI
accounts for the probability of variation in the succeeding ion from the ideal sequence. Because the sequence alternates, the PDF relevant to the next step alternates between cation–anion and cation–cation (both being tied to the initial reference cation and its ideal recipient). The first transfer from cation to anion would reference the cation–anion PDF, while the second transfer would refer to the cation–cation PDF, and so on. This assesses the likelihood of sequential cation–anion transfer in relation to the initial reference cation, calculated according to:
(19)

( r ) selects the PDF corresponding to the ideal recipient at each transfer interval. Specifically,
g

( r ) is evaluated in odd integer intervals of Δ r , and
g

(20)

In a regular structure where only ideal ions are present at the transfer position,
b NI
= 0 . The probability of coherence disruption rises as
b
NI
increases toward its maximum value of
b
NI
= 1 , where no ideal recipient ions are found at
r m
. The value of
b
NI
is typically low for the first transfer length in short-range-ordered molten salts, but quickly increases thereafter in the absence of regular structure.

The efficiency of vibrational energy transfer is proportional to the strength of the cation–anion bond. Maximum energy transfer efficiency occurs when thermal energy is transferred only via correlated vibrations, not through escaping ions that possess enough energy to leave semi-equilibrium positions. The effect of the bond strength on this behavior can be quantitatively observed in the PDF, where a non-zero minimum for the cation–anion pair following its first peak indicates the relative tendency for neighboring ions to dissociate. The ratio of the pair’s first peak height to the following minimum can be used as a relative scale for the bond strength factor,
b
KF
:
(21)
b
KF , c a
= 1 −
g

)
