)

Connected ion pair structures with the same resonant frequency are required for a correlated vibration to propagate. For an ideal, phonon-like energy carrier to remain coherent, a recipient ion must be bound by similar, oppositely charged ion structures in either direction parallel to propagation. A phonon transfer factor
b
PH
can be used to describe the potential inability of an ion chain to sustain a phonon. Differing masses and separation distances alter the bond strength and the resultant harmonic oscillator frequency of neighboring ions.

Assuming out-of-phase phonons cannot be sustained, only homogeneous counterions in an alternating chain are assumed to provide a coherent path for phonons. The term
b
PH
is thus a probability of the ideal cation–anion pair forming the succeeding chain segment. Because
b
NI
will account for the actual probability of encountering the ideal ion in the successive transfer,
b
PH
need only indicate whether the intervening ion is held in a semi-equilibrium position between similar cation–anion structures. The exact bond angle of the cation–anion chain is not considered here, assuming a sufficiently delocalized phonon depends principally on the structurally averaged alternating cation–anion homogeneity, with
b
NI
accounting for whether the energy carrier was actually received coherently or not by the next neighbor. In essence, having only a single type of cation and anion ensures that ions are held in the structure by pairs possessing similar resonant frequencies, so vibrational energy passed through ions is less likely to be dispersed by out-of-phase modes. Because this factor depends primarily on the concentration of the cation–anion species,
b
PH
is approximated as
(22)
b
PH,ca
= 1 −
x
c a
∑
i , j
x
c i
a j

Note that when there is only a single cation–anion pair that can be formed,
b
PH
= 0 , as is the case for all unary salts absent impurities. In this case, phonon propagation is not impeded by nonhomogeneous cation presence, only the probability of a cation–anion chain being ideally oriented and supported by sufficient bond strength (described by
b
NI
and
b
KF
). The phonon transfer factor requires only the molar fraction of the cation–anion of interest relative to all possible cation–anion pairs, and so is not radially-dependent. As with the bond strength factor,
b
PH
is applied in every transfer position.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Summary of the structural coherence model
ce:section.ce:para: The recommended coherence disruption factors represent first approximations of the discernible qualities from the PDF that may enable estimation of energy carrier structural coherence length. It is emphasized that while the limits of these factors represent physical bounds of influence on the prospective energy carrier coherence, the scaling behavior is assumed to be linear. Importantly, this estimate employs no adjusting terms, allowing the relative decoherence factors to control variability. There is also no consideration of relaxation time, leaving the question of whether structural disorder dominates decoherence before the mean free path can be affected by temporal changes to the structure.

These approximations provide an initial evaluation of the relevance of structural factors in determining energy carrier coherence and quantifying thermal conductivity using Eq. (23). Fig. 5 provides a conceptual diagram of each transfer factor related to the PDF and molten salt composition. An ideal, coherence-preserving case is depicted for each factor in Fig. 5(a)–(b), in comparison to a decoherence-inducing case in Fig. 5(d)–(f). For the non-ideal recipient disruption factor represented in Fig. 5(a) and (d),
b NI
increases when the ratio
g ca
( r )
/
g total
( r ) < 1 . The bond strength disruption factor (
b KF
) increases as the maximum peak and the following trough value of the cation–anion PDF approach each other, which yields complete decoherence of vibrational modes in the example PDF of a dilute gas PDF in Fig. 5(e), due to the absence of intermolecular forces. Fig. 5(c) depicts a correlated mode across two pairs enabled by consistent cation–anion alternation (minimizing
b PH
), contrasted by the de-correlated energy transfer caused by dissimilar cations in Fig. 5(f) which would increase
b NI
, proportional to the cation–anion pair concentration
x ca
.

The temperature dependence of
ℓ
sc
is less influential than for specific heat and sound velocity. The change in the first cation–anion peak position varies little with temperature, by less than − 0.4 % over 100 K temperature increase for 19 alkali halide salts modeled by Walz and Spoel [86]. In all of these salts, the cation–anion distance decreases with temperature, as has been observed for all cation–anion pairs in mixtures such as LiF–NaF–UF4 [119] and NaCl–KCl-ZnCl2 [91]. Notably, the homoionic bond distances tend to increase with temperature for these salts by an average of 0.6 % over 100 K for salts modeled by Walz and Spoel [86]. While the overall density of the salt decreases with temperature, the cation–anion bond length appears to shrink, with decreased peak magnitudes as well, while neighboring clusters distance themselves from one another. This adds more nuance to the typically proposed explanation for the temperature dependency, namely, that it is the separation of cation–anion clusters that reduces structural coherency beyond thermal expansion alone [68,120]. This observation predicts that the thermal expansion’s reduction of the population of energy carriers per unit volume and their effective velocity is much more significant than the reduction in mean free path, as is typically purported to principally account for the temperature dependency of thermal conductivity. Because temperature-related variation in the structural coherence length is minimal compared to the current model approximations used to calculate S ( r ) , the melting temperature estimation of
ℓ
sc
suits the purposes of the current model. The density-dependent
c p
and
v s
effectively account for most of the temperature-dependency, though a temperature-dependent correlation merits further development. One notable observation of the temperature-dependence is that every mechanism suggests a decreasing thermal conductivity with temperature, supporting Gheribi’s findings related to thermodynamic influence of thermal expansion on thermal conductivity [12].

The final calculation of thermal conductivity as a function of composition and temperature is given by
(23)
k ( x , T ) =
1 3
c
p , vol
( x , T )
v s
( x , T )
ℓ
sc
( x )

The expression above implies that the bulk properties of the mixture approximate the attributes of all energy transfer modes represented by each cation–anion pair. However, multiple energy transfer modes can clearly have different structural coherence lengths, and may have different effective heat capacities and group velocities. A rigorous description would resolve the effective partial contributions of each mode from the bulk properties. This is what is done in the spectral expression of phonon-mediated thermal conductivity, which necessitates the full resolution of frequency-dependent properties to then sum the parallel contributions of each mode. Depending on the difference between mode properties, the sum of parallel modes would differ from the product of the effective properties of all modes
( ∑ c v ℓ ≠ ∑ c ∑ v ∑ l )
.

While the mixture properties can be estimated using appropriate mixing rules of endmember values, simple mixing cannot recover the microscopic mode-specific contributions. This is because each cation–anion pair experiences a distinct local environment upon mixing that modifies its accessible microstates. What this means practically is that simple weighting may not describe the mixture-specific dynamics of each pair and would likely introduce more errors than can be addressed. The pair-specific heat capacity and group velocity of the microscopic fluxes for each species could be estimated using MD simulations [18], though such is beyond the practical scope of the current work. Resolving mode-specific contributions is thus left as an object of future study, with the current model incorporating the first-order estimate of the bulk properties to capture the combined effect of interacting transfer modes.

The central purpose of this study is to explore the validity of using the carrier-mediated thermal transport model by connecting the mean free path with a PDF-informed structural coherence length. The current work addresses this through two approaches. The first consists of a theoretical evaluation of the assumptions behind proposed molten salt thermal conductivity models, which leads to the revised mean free path term given by the structural coherence length. Predictions using the structural coherence model are then compared to experimental thermal conductivity values for a range of salts. This process provides an initial qualitative idea of which mechanisms in the model may contribute to its success or failure. The PDFs of 33 alkali, alkaline-earth, and actinide halide molten salt compositions were extracted from literature (listed in Table A.1) and used to predict the temperature-dependent thermal conductivity. These predictions were compared to the experimental measurement values for the 19 of these compositions for which measurement data exist. An analysis of model performance relative to differing salt structures and compositional variation is discussed. Prevailing patterns are highlighted regarding the limitations of the model in order to further illuminate the relationship between bulk properties and microscopic energy transfer behavior.

The analyzed compositions include unary salts, dissociating mixtures, and complex mixtures. Classification of molten salts as predominantly dissociating or complexing is based on the cation charge and resulting affinity for network formation. Monovalent alkali halides typically exhibit high ionic mobility with minimal network formation, and are thus considered to be generally dissociating [27,87]. As a conservative limit, complexation is inferred if the molar fraction of multivalent components exceeds > 10 %
[121,122], which includes the presence of alkaline earth cations such as Be
2 +
, Mg
2 +
, Ca
2 +
, and Sr
2 +
[94,123–125]. Actinides such as Th
4 +
and U
3 +
also induce network formation [126,127] but are distinguished separately as actinide-bearing mixtures.

In comparing prediction results to experimental data, it is worth reemphasizing the significant uncertainty in measurement values of thermal conductivity. Due to a lack of a standardized measurement method and uncertainty quantification, experimental thermal conductivity results can vary significantly across different methods [7]. Yang et al. provided a rigorous evaluation of molten salt thermal conductivity measurement data [51], which noted the probable overestimation of methods using the concentric cylinders approach or when radiative and convective contributions were not accounted for. A comprehensive uncertainty analysis of experimental data is beyond the scope of this work. A supplementary list of the measurement methods used to obtain the various datasets referenced is provided in the supplementary material in Appendix B, which includes the author and date of the measurements. This is to facilitate more critical evaluation of data reliability, given the variety of molten salt measurement methods and evolving interpretations of radiative and convective contributions. General trends in model agreement are evaluated with comments on experimental accuracy when appropriate.

To benchmark the current structural coherence model with prevailing predictive methods, the MAE and percentage of predicted values within the experimental error will be compared to the KTM predictions using Eq. (3). Since the calculation of the complexation rate ζ requires information about the specific coordination complexes present in the mixture [27], all predictions using the KTM in Eq. (3) are made assuming that ζ = 1 , which induces no additional amplification of the mass fluctuation term G ( x ) . This means that if ζ were to be determined for complexing mixtures, the predicted value would be further decreased. As will be shown in the comparisons made here, all complexing salts with the exception of LiF–BeF2 (67–33 mol %), otherwise known as FLiBe, already underpredict experimental values without the effect of ζ on Eq. (3). Both models also used the same input parameters (molar mass, density, sound velocity, specific heat, etc.). Measurement values for these quantities were used for mixtures where available, or otherwise extrapolated using the thermodynamic mixing rules summarized in Sections 3.2 and 3.3.

Because the experimental error of k measurements can reach 15–20% when uncertainty is rigorously calculated [8,9,67], predictions within 15% of the experimental result can be considered a good approximation. To quantify the maximum deviation that could be explained by the variability of parameters other than
ℓ
sc
, the propagated uncertainty for the product model of Eq. (23) can be used as an upper limit. The average reported uncertainty for specific heat and thermal conductivity in the MSTDB-TP [10] is 18.3% and 18.0%, respectively. Few sources of sound velocity cite experimental uncertainty, so the mean absolute error in extrapolated sound velocity from measured values is used instead. Comparing the estimated sound velocity to measured values for the four of the salts included in Table A.1, an average MAE of 19.95 % was obtained. The propagated total relative uncertainty considering these values is approximately 32.5 % . The following comparisons to data will thus be made in relation to an ideal ± 15 % experimental error range and a conservative estimated variability limit of ± 30 % , results outside of which are more likely to indicate a probable mechanistic explanation for the modeled discrepancy.

A significant limiting factor in this study remains the small number of salt compositions that have been both experimentally measured and evaluated as a PDF. Exhaustive validation will require additional measurements and modeling of key salts, particularly actinide-bearing mixtures, though the results of this work are hoped to encourage further studies of compositions of interest.

In Fig. 6 , the predicted thermal conductivities using the KTM and the present structural coherence model (SCM) are compared to experimental measurements for the 19 measured salts. Regions for an experimental deviation range of ± 15 % and a maximum variability range of ± 30 % are shown in the plot. Compared to the KTM, the SCM improves predictability in terms of MAE from 24.2% to 15.7%. For the limited available datasets, the SCM achieves a high level of agreement across the variety of simple, complex, and actinide-bearing salts, with larger deviations in several dissociating and complex mixtures. Of the 19 measured salts, 10 predicted by the SCM fall within experimental error, and 15 within the max variability. This is compared to KTM predictions, which give 6 and 11 predictions within experimental and max variability, respectively.

At first inspection, there appears to be minimal correlation between SCM deviations and the structural differences of the molten salts. Regarding complexing salts that include actinides, the KTM predicts the two NaCl–UCl3 mixtures with 56.3% MAE, while the SCM achieves 7.7% MAE for the salts. It is noted that the SCM predictions for the two NaCl–UCl3 compositions (37 mol % and 34.2 mol % UCl3) used the PDF obtained at 36 mol % UCl3, within 10% of the two measured compositions. The SCM performs similarly well for FLiBe (7.8% positive deviation) and unary MgCl2 (only 4.7% less than the experimental value). This is contrasted with several significant underestimations of complexing salts including CaCl2, NaCl–CaCl2 (49.03–50.97 mol %), and NaCl–MgCl2–CaCl2 (53.5–31.5–15.0 mol %) with respective deviations of 26.0%, 25.5%, and 27.4%. The largest deviation occurs for NaF–KF–MgF2 (53.5–31.5–15.0 mol %), which is underpredicted by 34.7%. For generally dissociating salts, LiCl–KCl (63.7–36.3 mol %) and NaCl–KCl (50–50 mol %) form the only two other mixtures analyzed that fall outside of the 30% deviation from the experimental measurement. The notable differences between the structural motifs and transport properties of these two groups of salts [128,129] obscure an obvious mechanistic reason for the SCM’s underestimations of particular salts.

Sources of variation in the SCM can be connected to individual terms by estimating their percentage contributions. Table 2 contains the percentage contributions of each term describing the energy carriers in the SCM, according to the calculation of
P i
described in Section 2.2.4 for the KTM and PGM. For the dataset included in Table A.1, the specific heat remained the dominant source of variability with
P sp
=
53.5
% , similar to that of the PGM (see Table 1). In contrast to the PGM and KTM, the mean free path term accounted for the next most significant source of variation with
P mfp
=
31.6
% . The velocity term caused the least variation, (
P v
=
14.8
% ), which lends confidence to model predictions despite the high degree of variation between measured sound velocity and values extrapolated from mixing rules.

Table 3 contains the input parameters and theoretical predictions for thermal conductivity using the structural coherence model in Eq. (23). The literature sources from which the PDFs were extracted are cited in Table A.1. The parameters for volumetric specific heat, sound velocity, and structural coherence length are extrapolated from mixing rules when the measured mixture values were unavailable. The percent deviations of the predicted thermal conductivity from the most reliable experimentally measured value available are also shown ( % Δ
k
exp
(
T min
) ). Because a given measurement temperature range may not coincide with the melting temperature, the linear correlation value of each dataset at the minimum measured temperature was used as the value for
k
exp
. When multiple datasets existed, the average of these values was obtained (taken at the maximum of the datasets’ minimum temperature point). No reliability weighting was applied to form reference correlations of multiple datasets, though reference should be made to Yang’s discussion of dataset reliability [51]. The source of each measurement value is noted in the final column.

Table 3 also includes the temperature-dependent slopes for
c p
and
v s
. The negative correlation of specific heat is due to the increasing density with temperature, which decreases the volumetric specific heat even when the molar specific heat is set as a constant. Because the volumetric specific heat and sound velocity are both linear functions of temperature, the predicted thermal conductivity is actually quadratic with respect to temperature. Due to typically very small slopes of
c p
and
v s
, the resultant curve for k using Eq. (23) appears extremely close to linear in the measured temperature range. Only the melting temperature thermal conductivity,
k 0
, is provided in Table 3, since all other parameters are included to recreate the temperature-dependent equation.

The mechanisms prescribed by the SCM can be evaluated on a structural level by analyzing patterns in the PDFs of various salt structures. The predicted structural coherence length can be directly compared to the expected mean free path by back-calculating from the measured thermal conductivity using the sound velocity and specific heat:
ℓ
exp
= 3
k
exp
/
c p
v s
. This is shown in Fig. 7 for 9 representative salts of the 33 studied. The PDF of each is overlaid with the radially-dependent survival probability functions ( S ( r ) ) and the predicted and back-calculated structural coherence lengths. To depict the reduction of energy carrier survival probability with distance, the area under S ( r ) for each pair is shaded proportionally. Fig. 7(a)–(c) consist of unary salts and include labels for the transfer positions
r m
on the top axis. Fig. 7(d)–(h) feature both dissociating and complexing molten salt mixtures. Fig. 7(i) shows the PDF of the actinide-bearing NaCl–UCl3 (64–36 mol %). The sources of the experimental values for each composition are provided in Table A.1. These observations highlight how mechanisms prescribed by the SCM map onto the PDF, compared to the experimentally inferred mean free path.

Several general observations are worth noting regarding the calculation of
ℓ sc
as shown in the plots of Fig. 7. The shape of S ( r ) shows the discrete positions at which β ( r ) is calculated, corresponding to the ideal transfer distances
r m
. Because no interactions occur up to the first peak, S ( r ) = 1 for 0 ≤ r ≤ Δ r , whereupon it drops at the first cation–anion peak position. Subsequent peaks in the PDF do not correspond to
r m
since these are the result of packing density and do not represent the most ideal positions for cation–anion energy transfer. Note that for the unary salts in Fig. 7(a)–(c), the most significant drop in S ( r ) occurs at the second transfer distance,
r
2
. This is due to the nonzero phonon transfer factor
b
PH
for mixtures, which interrupts correlated vibrations between subsequent pairs with differing cations. Consequently, mixtures experience the greatest drop at the first transfer distance
r 1
for each pair. This causes the average
ℓ sc
of mixtures to fall close to the weighted average peak position, but it is shifted slightly ahead because some coherence tends to persist past the first peak (as for Li–F in Fig. 7(f)).

Notably, the fact that
ℓ sc
for mixtures falls near the average first peak distance indicates that most coherent energy transfer paths end at the minimum transfer distance, where no phonon description is needed. The small degree to which S ( r ) persists following this position reflects the minimal contributions of collective vibrations due to the limited structural order. Unary salts exhibit nearly twice the mean free path, given the greater likelihood of sufficient cation–anion regularity to maintain correlated vibrational transfer beyond the interatomic distance. Only for unary salts does a considerable portion of the survival function S ( r ) persist to 2 π
/
Q lattice
, where a phonon can be meaningfully defined. These results support the view that most salts restrict phonon contributions, though their participation becomes significant in single cation–anion species salts. This highlights the ability of the model to account for the nonzero probability that some portion of energy carriers may remain coherent beyond the minimum interaction distance.

For the 19 measured salts, deviation between
ℓ
exp
and
ℓ
sc
exhibits a MAE of 16.1%. The model closely approximates the expected value for unary salts within ± 10 % for both the alkali halides KF and LiCl in Fig. 7(b)–(a) as well as the alkaline-earth MgCl2 in Fig. 7(c). The
ℓ
sc
of LiF–NaF–KF (46.5–11.5–42.0 mol %), otherwise known as FLiNaK, deviates from the expected value by only 2.8 % . While FLiNaK is nearly completely dissociating [147], FLiBe is characterized by significant complexation [93,131,148]. Despite the increased networK–Forming behavior, the predicted
ℓ
sc
of FLiBe is only 9.5 % greater than
ℓ
exp
(Fig. 7(f)). Similarly, for NaCl–UCl3, which is dominated by long oligomer networks when
x
UCl 3
> 30 % , the SCM predicts the back-calculated structural coherence length to within 11 % , below nominal experimental error. Note that Fig. 7(i) shows the expected
ℓ
exp
for NaCl–UCl3 to be slightly shorter than the average first peak position of Na–Cl and U–Cl, which is unrealistic considering the minimum wavelength requirement. This suggests a possible underestimated measured value used to calculate the back-calculated structural coherence length.

The agreement of the SCM for molten salt mixtures of diverse structural behaviors indicates that the effect of complex formation on coherence is more complicated than predicted by the degree of complexation alone (as suggested by the complexation rate ζ in the KTM [27]). Despite close predictions for FLiNaK, FLiBe, and NaCl–UCl3, the
ℓ
sc
underestimates
ℓ
exp
for NaCl–CaCl2 (Fig. 7(e)), NaF–KF–MgF2 (Fig. 7(g)), and NaCl–MgCl2–CaCl2 (Fig. 7(i)) by 24 % , 33 % , and 27 % , respectively. The expected back-calculated structural coherence length for these mixtures lies about three-quarters of the way between the average first cation–anion peak position and the average cation–cation peak position. While varying degrees of networking chains have been observed in Mg
2 +
and Ca
2 +
–containing mixtures, no long range order is apparent that would extend the phonon transfer factor [129,149]. NaF–KF–MgF2 (34.5–59.0–6.5 mol %), also known as FMgNaK, exhibited evidence of Mg–F–Mg chaining similar to Be–F–Be chaining in FLiBe [94], raising the question of what might cause the expected structural coherence length to be significantly farther relative to the cation–anion peaks in FMgNaK compared to FLiBe.

The accuracy of
ℓ
exp
in representing the mean free path depends on the information provided by the PDF. Due to the limited range of the PDF data of FLiNaK in Fig. 7(d) (provided in [24]), the second transfer point for the K–F pair is not included, and the S ( r ) : K--F remains at about 0.02. The early cutoff in the PDF range would decrease the area under S ( r ) and the resulting
ℓ sc
, though the difference is very minimal for this case. This remains a limitation of the model, however. As was the case for FLiNaK, the limited PDF range for FMgNaK in Fig. 7(g) cuts off the S ( r ) of the K–F pair before it decayed to approximately 0, though the low value of S ( r ) = 0.02 would not significantly increase the predicted
ℓ sc
to compensate for the underprediction. Potential physical mechanisms for deviations will be discussed below using NaCl–MgCl2–CaCl2 (53.5–31.5–15.0 mol %) as an example.

Compared to the PDF of FLiBe in Fig. 7(f), the PDF for NaCl–MgCl2–CaCl2 (53.5–31.5–15.0 mol %) shown in Fig. 7(h) displays a similar appearance of a strong initial cation–anion peak between the magnesium ion and chlorine, akin to the strong initial Be–F peak in FLiBe. The back-calculated
ℓ exp
=
3.43
in NaCl–MgCl2–CaCl2 corresponds to its much higher measured thermal conductivity than is predicted by the SCM. The KTM and PGM give mean free paths closer to
ℓ exp
(with
n
− 1
/
3
= 4.24 Å and
d
avg
= 2.73 Å, respectively), though as evidenced by the PDF, these exceed the minimum transfer distance which the two models assume to capture. If the sound velocity and measured specific heat accurately capture the energy carrier properties, some additional mechanism is required to extend the structural coherence length. No significant explanation appears obvious from the PDF itself in Fig. 7(h). If the structural coherence length were to increase, it would likely need to be in the context of an alternative energy carrier rather than correlated vibrational modes.

One distinguishing feature of magnesium in mixtures observed in MD simulations by Wu et al. is its tendency to form chain-like structures, where the polarizability of Cl
−
permits dipositive Mg
2 +
ions to form chains, rather than distinct complexes [48,95]. Jiang et al. observed the notable concentration of oligomers with increasing Mg
2 +
content [150], including the linkage of Mg–Mg ions in corner-sharing complexes [123]. This contrasts with the complex-forming charge alternation observed with increasing concentration of BeF2 in FLiBe [93]. While an alternating chain is ideal for phonon transfer, the disorder beyond the first bond dampens this quickly. If the Mg
2 +
ions minimize the screening effect on Mg–Mg interactions, cation–cation energy transfer may form a non-negligible pathway for energy carriers, depending on the structures involved [148].

It is worth calling into question the accuracy of the other measured parameters,
c p
and
v s
, in capturing the energy carrier attributes, as well as the reliability of the measured thermal conductivity. To qualitatively inspect the validity of model parameters, the experimentally measured sound velocity or specific heat (measured for the mixture or estimated from its components) can be compared to the back-calculated value (
v

or

) predicted by the model and the experimental thermal conductivity, respectively solved using

and

= 3

/

ℓ

. In effect, this isolates the variability in an individual parameter assuming all other terms accurately describe the energy carrier. Fig. 8 provides a parity plot of the velocity (a) and specific heat (b) terms, with the x-axis representing the measured value and the y-axis as the back-calculated value required for the model to match the measured thermal conductivity.

Considering possible deviations of energy carrier velocity from the standard speed of sound used in the model, little evidence currently exists to support alternative interpretations. If the presence of multiple homogeneously mixed cation species limits the extent of “fast sound”-like dynamics as discussed in Section 3.3, this would cause the dispersion of mixtures to more closely approximate the bulk sound velocity due to irregularity of the cation subcomponent. If unary salts are unique in this regard, these would be better described by the liquid metal sound velocity. To the authors’ knowledge, no dispersion relations have been attained for multi-component mixtures, which are needed to verify whether high-frequency group velocities can better describe energy carrier propagation and, if present in particular mixture types, account for the underprediction.

To provide an estimate of the change induced by a fast-sound correction applied to cation–anion chains, this can be done using the sound velocity of a salt’s liquid metal counterpart per the suggested mechanism of Demmel et al. [106]. Applying this to the unary chloride and fluoride salts of Li, Na, K, Mg, Ca, and Sr with their respective liquid metal sound velocities (estimated from density correlations at the melting point [151]) yields an overestimation of
k exp
by 41%. In contrast, unary salts (including MgCl2) are well-predicted using
v s
in the present model, with an average underestimation of −7%. It may be argued that the increased velocity in unary salts could replace the phonon transfer factor, which is what most contributes to the longer mean free path of unary salts. If the first peak distance were used instead along with the liquid metal sound velocity,
k exp
is still underestimated by −22%.

There may be some contribution of higher group velocities, but the questionable projection of a cation–cation mechanism to cation–anion dynamics and the relatively large uncertainty in liquid metal sound velocity require further validation before it can add mechanistic clarity to the current engineering approach. In the case of NaCl–MgCl2–CaCl2 in Fig. 8, the group velocity at melting temperature would need to exceed 2877 m/s (compared to the measured
v
s , exp
= 2 , 096 m/s) to match the experimentally measured thermal conductivity extrapolated from its components. If the velocity is accurately predicted by
v
s
, other underestimations may be responsible.

Because experimental measurements of specific heat capture all energy storage modes, some of which may be non-propagating (and thus do not contribute to thermal conductivity), this provides an upper bound for the actual volumetric heat capacity per volume of energy carriers (see Section 3.2). In Fig. 8(b), this should correspond to the experimental value exceeding the back-calculated model term (
c
p , exp
≥
c
p , model
). Surprisingly, this is true for only 7 of the 19 measured salts. The upper limit imposed by the specific heat is the principal reliable bound of the model [101]; energy carriers cannot access more energy storage modes than those available in the degrees of freedom of the system. For NaCl–MgCl2–CaCl2 (53.5–31.5–15.0 mol %), the measured specific heat reported by Wei et al. [70] is 96% of the extrapolated value from molar mixing. This indicates that the measured specific heat is unlikely to be an underestimation. In the absence of a current explanation for a significantly increased structural coherence length and the unverified fast sound needed to compensate, the other possibility is that the measured thermal conductivity by Rong referenced in [70,71] was an overestimation of the actual thermal conductivity.

Other limitations that may contribute to the discrepancy between measured and modeled results for mixtures include the fact that no weighting was applied to pair-specific contributions. If energy carriers predominantly survive across a single cation–anion transfer length in mixtures, it is unlikely that the energy transferred and effective transfer velocity are consistent across different species of cation–anion pairs. The reduced mass of the ion pair provides a simple scale to the vibrational energy stored, which would vary between differently massed ions and their relative bond strengths. The sound velocity also varies nonlinearly with changing mass disparity between cation and anion [117]. As noted in Section 3.4.5, neglecting this weighting would lead to different predictions of thermal conductivity if the partial contributions of each endmember to specific heat or sound velocity vary significantly. While the bond strength may be estimated using Eq. (21) to give relative portions of specific heat mediated by each endmember, estimating the effective transfer velocity in each pair is less straightforward. Adaptation of the model to accommodate the potential for pair-specific contributions to specific heat and velocity will be addressed in future studies.

The predicted thermal conductivity of LiCl as a function of temperature is shown in Fig. 9 , as compared to Gheribi’s KTM predictions and measurement data by Nagasaka [60]. Both models exhibit a negative temperature dependence, though the SCM provides a steeper negative slope closer to the linear fit of [60]. The negative dependency of the SCM is two-fold: the sound velocity and the density-dependent volumetric specific heat, both of which decrease with temperature according to most measured values. The change in density with temperature decreases the number of ions per unit volume, and because the model assumes that energy carriers traverse via the cation–anion bond, the decrease in energy carriers per unit volume is proportional. Decreasing the density thus reduces the volumetric number of energy carriers involved, represented by
c p
. The sound velocity temperature dependence comes solely from the correlation used. If the sound velocity accurately captures the microscopic energy carrier group velocity, then the decreasing sound velocity with temperature should be accounted for in addition to the decrease in energy carriers involved (accounted for in
c p
). As noted previously, the combination of these linear terms in Eq. (23) produces a quadratic temperature dependence of k .

Fig. 10 presents the KTM and SCM predictions for FLiNaK with a few of the most presented measurement values. Predictions have been made with experimental sound velocity and specific heat data of FLiNaK (indicated by “Mix Data”), in comparison with simple mixing rules, to depict the uncertainty inherent in the mixture-extrapolated values. One deviation between the calculated prediction of KTM shown here and previous results by Gheribi [27] may be due to the use of Robertson’s published sound velocity correlation for FLiNaK [13], whereas previous KTM predictions of FLiNaK used compressibility measurements [152]. Near the melting point, the predictions by SCM using mixture values for
c p
and
v s
[10] match closely with the average minimum-temperature measured values. A highly recognized mixture, measurements of FLiNaK still raise questions regarding the source of its temperature dependency. While the results of Merritt [67] and Rudenko [68] show a negative correlation, the results by Gallagher [55] and Robertson [13] depict a positive trend (however, Gallagher notes that radiative heat transfer may be a confounding variable in the measurement). Every mechanism of the SCM model predicts a decreasing temperature dependence, including the slight but consistent reduction of the cation–anion PDF peak distance which would reduce the calculated structural coherence length. While Robertson’s measurements show the tightest correlation, the associated temperature range is limited, pointing to the need for accurate measurements across a large temperature range to verify these modeled mechanisms in experiment.

Fig. 11 presents the temperature-dependent thermal conductivity predictions for FLiBe compared to measurement data [64–66]. As previously stated, the KTM model calculations in this work did not use the complexation rate modifier ζ , which would have reduced the predicted result for KTM. The plotted correlation does approach the value presented for Gheribi’s KTM in [153] of k = 1.56
W
m
− 1
K
− 1
at 800 K, though that result was presented before the latest complexation rate term was introduced [27]. The main purpose of this plot is to show the agreement of the structural coherence model predictions with measured values. Measurements by Kato [65] and Redkin [64] both come from thermal diffusivity measurements combined with specific heat and density values to obtain thermal conductivity [154]. Bobrova obtained direct thermal conductivity measurements using the concentric cylinders technique [66], though this method has been questioned for its ability to control alternative heat transfer modes and consistent positive temperature dependence [51]. The thermal conductivity predicted by the structural coherence model has a slightly steeper slope than both Kato’s linear correlation and Gheribi’s or the ideal mixture relation.

Fig. 12 depicts the predicted thermal conductivity of NaCl–UCl3 as a function of temperature. Mixture properties for specific heat [73] and density [144] were used, though no sound velocity data were found for the mixture. The SCM prediction displays the negative trend similar to the measurements of Termini, which differ from the correlation given by Rose. Termini applied the variable gap apparatus to obtain steady-state thermal conductivity measurements, which exhibit more scatter but have been shown to account for convection and radiative effects [72,155]. Laser flash analysis (LFA) used by Rose provides a strong correlation, though other LFA systems have exhibited confounding conduction effects in the measurement apparatus as highlighted elsewhere [13].

Though the experimental correlations were obtained for slightly differing compositions (within ± 5 % molar fraction UCl3), the SCM prediction displays very close agreement where the correlations of Termini and Rose converge near the melting point [72,73]. While NaCl–UCl3 was noted for the possible presence of connected cation–cation chains because
r
cc , max
< 2
r
ca , max
[96], the strong agreement suggests that the effect was either negligible or NaCl provided a sufficient spacing effect to interrupt cation–cation correlations [97]. Referring again to the PDF of NaCl–UCl3 in Fig. 7(i), the survival function S ( r ) decays almost completely at the first transfer distance for U–Cl, preempting any possible correlation between adjacent cation–anion complexes.

The KTM prediction for NaCl–UCl3 is particularly low, with
k
KTM
= 0.114
W
m
− 1
K
− 1
at the melting temperature. If calculated only using the kinetic part of Eq. (2) (without the mass fluctuation modifier
G i
( x ) ), the KTM gives
k
KTM
= 0.192
W
m
− 1
K
− 1
, which is closer but still underestimates the measured value. The significant deviation caused by G ( x ) points to the physically unrealistic factor played by the modifier. As with the other KTM calculations included in this work, the KTM result plotted in Fig. 12 ignored the complexation factor by setting ζ ( x , T ) = 1 ; this would have further decreased the predicted thermal conductivity due to the high fraction of U atoms involved in polymerization above 30% mol fraction of UCl3 [121].

Fig. 13 provides the temperature-dependent predictions of the thermal conductivities of various unmeasured salts, including 5 actinide-bearing compositions. Of note are the particularly high thermal conductivities predicted for ThF4 and UF4. Considering the substantial negative excess thermal conductivity in the modeled LiF–NaF–UF4 (54.54-36.36-9 mol %), where unary LiF and NaF have predicted melting thermal conductivities of 1.33 and 0.89
W
m
− 1
K
− 1
, respectively, it would at first seem that a lower unary k value for UF4 is needed to contribute to the lower mixture thermal conductivity. According to the SCM, the much higher unary values are due to the phonon transfer factor, which posits that the mechanism responsible for increased unary thermal conductivity is the greater likelihood of repeating cation–anion sequences in unary salts. When multiple cation species are present in mixtures, this decreases the probability of ideal sequences for coherent phonon mode transfer. Additional coherence factors inherent in actinide mixtures are predicted to be encoded in the PDF, including, for example, the decreased U-ligand distances observed to result in transient covalency of UCl3 [156]. Experimental measurements would provide substantial insights regarding the validity of the modeled phonon transfer mechanism, although this is complicated by the high melting temperatures of ThF4 and UF4.

The present description of thermal conductivity using the structural coherence model to describe energy carrier mean free path lengths matches theoretical correlations with a negative slope as a function of temperature as previously proposed [12,14,37]. Rather than being linear, using temperature-dependent volumetric specific heat and the temperature-dependent sound velocity gives the predicted correlation a negative quadratic shape. Both of these are related to the thermal expansion of the lattice, which reduces the energy carriers per unit volume (reducing specific heat) and decreases the bulk modulus and resultant speed of sound. The relative success of the current model in predicting melting point thermal conductivities suggests that these properties bear an important relation to participating energy carriers. Whether these scale with temperature according to the density-dependent specific heat and sound velocity is an important piece of information needed to verify the validity of these parameters in the energy carrier picture. The relatively steeper slope predicted by the model using Eq. (23) has implications for predicting changes in heat transfer behavior in MSRs. Since the slope of the present model is dependent on density and sound velocity data with non-negligible uncertainty, it may benefit from relying on thermodynamic scaling similar to Gheribi’s approach to account for the influence of density on thermal conductivity, but applied to both parameters. Temperature-dependent functions applied to the model will be a subject of future studies.

The most practical limitation of the present structural coherence model is its dependence on accurate partial pair distribution functions of the molten salt structure. While the combined PDF can be obtained experimentally, the partial PDF is nearly always derived via classical MD or AIMD simulations, from which a simulated combined PDF is often needed to verify that the partial components replicate the combined system [24]. Because the partial PDFs cannot be extracted from the total experimental PDF, simulations remain the only practical method of obtaining usable PDFs for SCM calculations. Part of the value of the SCM lies in the fact that MD calculations of thermal conductivity require orders of magnitude more time than what is required to obtain the PDF, enabling more rapid PDF collection of varied compositions once interatomic potentials are optimized. As mentioned previously, the Green-Kubo method of thermal conductivity calculation is not without significant uncertainty [27]. Green-Kubo Modal Analysis has demonstrated increased accuracy in resolving frequency-specific vibrational contributions for amorphous solids, but requires the absence of ion diffusion which defines a liquid [157].

The assumption of linear factors is another limitation of the present model. Multi-component mixtures do not figure into the calculation of disruption factors in Eq. (18), which is performed for each endmember in parallel and then linearly combined into a mixture
ℓ
sc , i
according to molar composition (Eq. 3.4). While this already represents a potential source of error since the disruption factors themselves may vary nonlinearly (Section 3.4.5), the presence of mixtures may introduce complex interaction dynamics. For example, the phonon transfer factor
b PH
assumes decoherence proportional to the presence of any dissimilar cation–anion species, though it is still possible for differing ion mass arrangements to form overlapping vibrational levels, effectively permitting the possibility of extended collective vibrations through heterogeneous ionic structures. These questions represent important areas for more thorough characterization of composition-dependent ion dynamics, which may improve the predictability for currently underestimated mixtures.

Notably, the current model does not include explicit time dependence and therefore neglects ion-pair transitions arising from diffusion or advection. To provide a qualitative comparison of these processes, the characteristic lifetime of the energy carrier can be contrasted with estimated timescales for ion-pair rearrangements involving a highly mobile ion, Li
+
, in several representative molten salts. The effective energy carrier lifetime,
τ c
, may be approximated by the average distance traveled (typically no more than the second transfer distance 2 Δ r ; see Fig. 7) divided by the carrier velocity
v s
:
(24)
τ c
≈ 2 Δ r
/
v s
The timescale between diffusive ion-hopping events,
τ diff
, can be estimated from the diffusion coefficient D :
(25)
τ diff
≈
Δ
r 2
6 D
An upper-bound estimate for advection-driven ion-pair transitions,
τ adv
, may be obtained from the interatomic spacing and the maximum mean velocity expected in an MSR loop (
v fluid
= 4.5 m/s [162]):
(26)
τ adv
≈
Δ r
v fluid
Using the maximum reported self-diffusion coefficients of Li
+
in LiF, LiCl, FLiNaK (46.5–11.5–42 mol %), and FLiBe (66–34 mol %) [146], these characteristic timescales follow the ordering
τ c
<
τ diff
<
τ adv
for all salts examined. For the unary salts, LiF and LiCl exhibit
τ diff
of 0.33 ps and 0.61 ps, respectively, compared to energy carrier lifetimes of
τ c
= 0.15 ps and 1.7 ps. The gap is substantially greater in the mixtures: in FLiBe,
τ diff
= 170 ps while
τ c
= 0.12 ps; in FLiNaK,
τ diff
= 3.1 ps while
τ c
= 0.23 ps. The advection-inferred timescale is much longer than the carrier lifetime in all cases, falling within 34 ps <
τ adv
< 53 ps .

These comparisons indicate that, even for an especially mobile species such as Li
+
, structural decoherence appears to be predominantly responsible for limiting energy carrier mean free paths at the short length scales typical of molten salts. Diffusion-driven pair rearrangements may contribute non-negligibly to decoherence in some unary salts, but remain subdominant. Leveraging time-dependent information in the VHF could evaluate the errors inherent in these approximations. Including a time-dependent probability of decoherence could only reduce the calculated structural coherence length, and is left to be incorporated in future model development.

Another significant hurdle to validating model predictions is the notable uncertainty and lack of thermal conductivity measurement data. Notably, only two measurements of actinide-bearing salts had an associated PDF to enable data comparison. Further validation of the model performance relative to actinides necessitates additional measurements, which may include the actinide mixtures listed in Table 3. Improvements to existing methods of measurement toward a standardized approach could lead to better comparisons between datasets. Of particular importance related to validation with measurements are datasets that span a compositional variety of a salt system and a broad temperature range. Particularly when conducted using the same measurement system, these can reveal patterns in compositional and temperature dependency as predicted by the model. This may confirm theoretical predictions of the negative temperature dependence of thermal conductivity, though the resolution required to confirm the nonlinear trend predicted by the model is challenged by current measurement uncertainty. A sufficiently wide temperature range for such validation becomes more difficult due to material challenges and increasing radiative and convective contributions with temperature, emphasizing the need for high-temperature and well-characterized thermal conductivity systems.

By using the pair distribution function (PDF) to approximate the degree of short-range order, the structural coherence model (SCM) provides an informed mean free path for improved molten salt thermal conductivity predictions. The modeled mechanisms predict a negative dependence of thermal conductivity on temperature. The SCM achieved improved predictive capability for 19 analyzed salts compared to previous kinetic theory approaches, with a mean absolute error of 15.7%. Of the 19 modeled salts, 15 fit within a 30% deviation attributable to errors in model parameters and measured thermal conductivity. The model generally attains close approximations for both actinide and dissociating mixtures, including unary salts. The mean free paths predicted by the SCM imply a minimal contribution of collective modes in most mixtures, though the model suggests their more significant role in unary salts.

Uncertainty in thermal conductivity measurements and lack of data limit model validation, though compositional studies using consistent measurement methods can potentially verify trends predicted by the model. Future studies seek to further resolve pairwise contributions in multi-component mixtures and better inform the currently linear mixture and disruption weighting. Connecting the mean free path to the PDF facilitates the feasible use of machine-learned potentials and PDF predictions to directly predict the structural coherence length, accelerating database development without relying exclusively on thermal conductivity measurements.

By relating the short-range-ordered structure to the mean free path of energy carriers, the present model provides practical validation of the kinetic theory of energy carriers applied to liquids. This bears implications for molecular energy transport theory of liquids in general. This work’s contributions to structurally based theoretical models of thermal conductivity are aimed at advancing the development of MSR technologies, enabling them to play a valuable role in the future.
